subject: mathematics
prose_summary:
Section 14.1 constructs a family of geometric figures with a ruler and compass and justifies each construction through congruence. Starting from the Grade 6 'Eyes' design, it shows that the two arc-centres lie at equal distances from the endpoints X and Y of a supporting segment, defines bisection and the perpendicular bisector, and constructs the perpendicular bisector by drawing equal-radius arcs above and below XY and joining their intersections. It justifies this rigorously: showing ∆ABX ≅ ∆ABY (SSS) and then ∆AOX ≅ ∆AOY (SAS) proves O is the midpoint and AB is perpendicular to XY, and it establishes the general fact that any point equidistant from X and Y lies on the perpendicular bisector. It also shows how eyes of different shapes arise by choosing different pairs of points on that bisector as arc-centres, and stresses that this compass method of finding a midpoint is more accurate than measuring with a scale. Figure it Out tasks probe whether equal radii and both-side arcs are really necessary. The method is extended to construct a 90° angle at a point on a line by treating that point as the midpoint of a segment and drawing the perpendicular bisector through it, needing only one pair of arcs since the point is already known to lie on the bisector. A historical passage describes how the Vedic Śulba-Sūtras — geometric texts of the Vedic period used to lay out fire altars, and part of the Vedāṅgas — construct the same perpendicular bisector with a stretched rope (Kātyāyana-Śulbasūtra 1.2), using pegs at X and Y and the rope's midpoint pulled taut above and below. The section then develops angle bisection — drawing congruent triangles ∆OBC ≅ ∆OAC (SSS) so that OC bisects ∠AOB — giving a 45° angle by bisecting a right angle, and a general method to copy an angle by transferring an isosceles triangle's base (∆ABC ≅ ∆XYZ by SSS). This 45° angle is exactly what the eight-fold petal design needs, since 360° divided into eight equal parts gives 45°. Copying an angle underlies the construction of a line parallel to a given line: a transversal is drawn, and the corresponding angle at a chosen point is copied so that the new line makes equal corresponding angles with the transversal and is therefore parallel. The section also constructs decorative arches — a trefoil arch from equal angles and equal arms at two points, and a pointed arch from two equal segments and their midpoints. Using a 60° angle built from an equilateral triangle (two equal-radius arcs from the ends of a segment), the section constructs a regular hexagon, first reasoning that six congruent equilateral triangles can meet at a single centre precisely because their 60° angles sum to 360° and so cover the region around the point without gaps or overlaps, giving a hexagon with equal sides and 120° angles. From these it derives 30° and 15° angles by further bisection, a 120° angle, and a 6-pointed star whose six points are equilateral triangles. Its many Figure it Out sets ask students to construct angles and their bisectors, parallel-line pairs, an 8-petalled figure, trefoil and pointed arches, an inflexed arc, an optical-illusion figure, and a perpendicular to a line through an external point.

Section 14.2 explores tiling — covering a region without gaps or overlaps — and reasons about when it is possible. It opens with the Chinese tangram, whose seven pieces (from a divided square) are rearranged into figures, then asks whether rectangular grids can be tiled by 2 × 1 tiles. It shows a 4 × 6 grid can be tiled, and reasons that a 5 × 7 grid (35 unit squares) cannot, since each tile covers exactly two squares and 35 is odd. It develops general strategies for m × n grids by the parities of m and n — a grid with an even number of rows can be tiled column by column — and examines grids with a unit square removed, asking when the remaining even-count region is tileable. It then introduces a powerful black-and-white colouring argument: colouring the grid like a chessboard, each 2 × 1 tile must cover exactly one black and one white square, so any tileable region must have equal numbers of black and white squares; the region in Fig. 6.14, with 8 white and 6 black squares, therefore can never be tiled — a striking case where deliberately making the problem more complicated by adding colour actually makes it easier to settle. It then turns to tiling the entire plane, showing squares, equilateral triangles, and regular hexagons all work, noting tilings with multiple and non-regular shapes including M. C. Escher's animal tilings and a tiling discovered only in 2023, and observing the hexagonal cells of bee hives and wasp nests as natural tilings. Its Figure it Out sets ask whether particular region-and-tile combinations can be tiled.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 14.1 p.140
enumerated_exercises.description: When constructing the perpendicular bisector, explore through construction whether the arcs above and below XY must have the same radius, then justify your answer.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 14.1 p.140
enumerated_exercises.description: Explore whether both pairs of arcs must be on opposite sides of XY, or whether both pairs can be on the same side, and justify your answer.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 14.1 p.140
enumerated_exercises.description: Explore whether the two arcs of one intersecting pair must use the same radius, and justify your answer.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 14.1 p.140
enumerated_exercises.description: Recreate the given design using only a ruler and compass, then trace its boundary to make it stand out.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 14.1 p.142
enumerated_exercises.description: Justify why AB in Fig. 6.4 (the rope construction from the Śulba-Sūtras) is the perpendicular bisector of XY.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 14.1 p.142
enumerated_exercises.description: Devise different methods to construct a 90° angle at a given point on a line using a rope.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 14.1 p.144
enumerated_exercises.description: Construct at least 4 different angles and draw their bisectors.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 14.1 p.144
enumerated_exercises.description: Construct the 8-petalled figure shown in Fig. 6.5 (dividing 360° into eight 45° angles).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 14.1 p.144
enumerated_exercises.description: In Step 2 of angle bisection, if arcs of equal radius are drawn on the other side, explore through construction whether OC is still an angle bisector, and justify your answer.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 14.1 p.144
enumerated_exercises.description: Identify the other angles that can be constructed using angle bisection, and decide whether a 65.5° angle can be constructed.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 14.1 p.144
enumerated_exercises.description: Come up with a method to construct the angle bisector using a rope.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 14.1 p.144
enumerated_exercises.description: Construct the given petal figure so that the petals are of the maximum possible size within a given square.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 14.1 p.146
enumerated_exercises.description: Construct at least 4 different angles in different orientations without taking any measurement, and make a copy of each using ruler and compass.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 14.1 p.146
enumerated_exercises.description: Construct Fig. 6.6, a repeating unit made by copying an angle in two orientations.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 14.1 p.148
enumerated_exercises.description: Construct 4 pairs of parallel lines in different orientations using ruler and compass.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 14.1 p.148
enumerated_exercises.description: Construct the given figure (the symmetric S–T–…–W frame) using parallel-line constructions.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 14.1 p.151
enumerated_exercises.description: Use the support lines in Fig. 6.11 to construct a pointed arch, making different arches by changing the radius of the arcs.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 14.1 p.151
enumerated_exercises.description: Make your own arch designs.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 14.1 p.154
enumerated_exercises.description: Construct the given figures, including an inflexed arc and figure (b) which can be constructed using only a compass.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 14.1 p.154
enumerated_exercises.description: Examine the given optical-illusion figure, explain how the effect happens, and recreate it in your notebook.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 14.1 p.155
enumerated_exercises.description: Construct the given figure by first finding the angles in it.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 14.1 p.155
enumerated_exercises.description: Draw a line l and a point P outside it, and construct a perpendicular to l through P (using a segment on l whose perpendicular bisector passes through P).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 14.2 p.156
enumerated_exercises.description: Rearrange the seven tangram pieces to form each of the given figures.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 14.2 p.160
enumerated_exercises.description: Decide whether the given region can be tiled with the given tile.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 14.2 p.160
enumerated_exercises.description: Decide whether the second given region can be tiled with the given tile.
